critic_raw:
This multi-modal immunotherapy response prediction design has a critical sample size problem that invalidates most of the evaluation strategy, plus a temporal confound that is rationalized away without sufficient justification.

With n=500 total patients split 300/100/100, the test set contains 100 patients. Given the stated ~40-50% response rate typical in melanoma immunotherapy (CR+PR rates in checkpoint inhibitor trials), the positive class in the test set is approximately 40-50 patients. AUROC and AUPRC confidence intervals on a 100-patient binary classification test set are extremely wide: a 95% confidence interval for AUROC on 100 patients with balanced classes spans roughly ±0.10 AUROC units. This means a multi-modal model that achieves 0.75 AUROC is statistically indistinguishable from one achieving 0.65 AUROC. The design reports bootstrap confidence intervals but does not pre-specify a minimum detectable effect size or power calculation, so there is no guarantee the experiment is powered to detect the effect it claims to test.

The stratified random split — rather than temporal split — is justified on the grounds that 'temporal ordering reflects documentation sequence, not predictive information flow.' This argument is flawed. In a retrospective cohort, patients treated in earlier years may have received different immunotherapy protocols, different RECIST assessment practices, or different second-line therapies that affect response classification. Training on earlier patients and testing on later ones would expose temporal distribution shift; the stratified random split hides this confound by mixing early and late patients across folds, allowing the model to 'learn' from contemporaneous cases.

The baseline model uses genomic features only ('identical in architecture but uses genomic features only, excluding clinical tabular and text branches'). This creates a confound: the multimodal model has more parameters (additional branches) than the baseline. Even with identical regularization, more parameters means more optimization freedom, so the performance gap could reflect capacity difference rather than multi-modal complementarity. A proper ablation would hold total parameter count fixed while varying input modalities.

BioBERT embeddings of pathology reports and treatment summaries are 768-dimensional per patient. With 300 training patients and 768 text features (plus genomic and clinical features), the multimodal model is severely underdetermined. The text branch's FC layer on top of BioBERT adds additional parameters. Without aggressive regularization or dimensionality reduction, the text branch will overfit to specific vocabulary patterns in the training reports rather than clinical content.
all_issues_raised: The test set contains only ~100 patients, yielding AUROC confidence intervals of approximately ±0.10, which means the experiment is almost certainly underpowered to distinguish the multi-modal model from the genomic-only baseline; no power calculation or minimum detectable effect size is pre-specified.

The stratified random split obscures temporal treatment protocol drift: patients treated in earlier years may have received different immunotherapy regimens and RECIST assessment practices, and mixing them across folds allows the model to train on contemporaneous cases while concealing this distribution shift.

The baseline model (genomic features only) uses a reduced architecture compared to the multimodal model, creating a parameter count confound; any performance gap could reflect increased model capacity rather than multi-modal signal complementarity.

BioBERT produces 768-dimensional embeddings per patient, and with only 300 training patients the text branch is severely underdetermined; without explicit dimensionality reduction prior to the FC layer, the text branch will overfit to specific note-writing patterns in the training set rather than generalizable clinical content.
all_issues_adjudicated: The test set contains only ~100 patients, yielding AUROC confidence intervals of approximately ±0.10, which means the experiment is almost certainly underpowered to distinguish the multi-modal model from the genomic-only baseline; no power calculation or minimum detectable effect size is pre-specified.

The stratified random split obscures temporal treatment protocol drift: patients treated in earlier years may have received different immunotherapy regimens and RECIST assessment practices, and mixing them across folds allows the model to train on contemporaneous cases while concealing this distribution shift.

The baseline model (genomic features only) uses a reduced architecture compared to the multimodal model, creating a parameter count confound; any performance gap could reflect increased model capacity rather than multi-modal signal complementarity.

BioBERT produces 768-dimensional embeddings per patient, and with only 300 training patients the text branch is severely underdetermined; without explicit dimensionality reduction prior to the FC layer, the text branch will overfit to specific note-writing patterns in the training set rather than generalizable clinical content.
